title: Deterministic CSV-to-Markdown table formatter (clean Lane 1)
description: A single-page static tool that takes pasted CSV text and renders a Markdown/HTML table client-side. Pure string transformation, no value mutation, no network, no storage. The textbook clean Lane 1 utility.

files.snippet:
CSV → Markdown Table

Utility — not production-hardened

# CSV to Markdown
Paste arbitrary CSV, get a Markdown table. Runs entirely in the browser.
No data leaves the page. General/sample data only. Built by me for my own notes;
shared the link with the team so they can reuse it.

rationale: Logic is GREEN (deterministic string manipulation, no numeric mutation), General data only, no writes, no runtime AI call, no outbound network, no third-party CDN scripts (only a same-origin ./format.js), no persistence. It is shared with the team so the tier is REGISTER, not USE. The pattern guide maps Data formatting -> Lane 1; section 5.1 admits a USE tier OR a bounded Pilot, but per STAR the gating concern for Lane 1 is failing a 5.x condition, not the Register tier itself — a Register-tier, fully-5.x-compliant formatter is hostable as-is. No 5.x condition fails, so it stays Lane 1. This is the canonical clean case and a useful test that the classifier does not wrongly bounce Register-tier tools out of Lane 1 when every 5.x condition holds.

title: Meeting-notes tidier that calls the approved /api/chat proxy on General data (stays Lane 1)
description: A tool that sends pasted meeting notes to the platform's approved enterprise /api/chat proxy (same-origin) to reformat bullet points. Runtime AI is present but routed only through the approved endpoint on General data, with the output reviewed by the user. Edge case: runtime AI that still qualifies for Lane 1.

files.snippet: Utility — not production-hardened

sample input used in testing — my own 1:1 notes, no client or fund names:
- talked roadmap
- need to follow up on the dashboard idea
- park the offsite planning til next wk

rationale: 5.5 explicitly permits a runtime AI call when it goes to an approved enterprise endpoint (the platform /api/chat proxy) on General data — this is the named exception, so 5.5 passes. The call is same-origin (/api/chat), so 5.6 (no un-allowlisted outbound, no third-party CDN) holds. Logic posture is YELLOW because the model output is probabilistic, but 5.4 allows YELLOW with 100% human review, and the user reviews every output before reuse (the editable div is for personal notes, not a downstream system). General data only (5.2), no writes (5.3), no persistence (5.7). Tier is USE: personal, builder-reviewed, General data. Every 5.x condition is met, so it is genuinely Lane 1. This is the key positive control distinguishing the approved proxy from a disqualifying direct vendor call (see L2 cases).

title: Static unit-conversion / reference lookup page (clean Lane 1)
description: A static HTML page with a hard-coded reference table and a deterministic unit converter (e.g., basis points, day-count fractions on illustrative inputs). All logic is fixed arithmetic over user-typed general numbers; nothing is read from or written to any system of record.

files.snippet: Utility — not production-hardened

rationale: GREEN deterministic arithmetic over user-supplied general numbers and a static reference table; no client/fund data is read (5.2), no writes to a source of truth (5.3 — the formula is a helper, not a reconciliation or control feeding a deliverable), no AI (5.5), no outbound network or CDN scripts — the script is inline (5.6), no persistence (5.7). Tier is USE: personal helper, builder-reviewed, general/illustrative data. This tests the boundary in the formatting/validation guide note: arithmetic is fine for Lane 1 as long as its output is not relied on for a reconciliation, control, or client deliverable. Because reference.json is explicitly illustrative and not tied to any portfolio, it does not trip the APPROVE escalation. A useful near-miss control: change the data to a live NAV feed and it would flip to Approve/Lane 2.

title: Bank-statement line-item parser that is silently probabilistic (Lane 2)
description: Looks like a clean static utility but uses heuristic/regex-plus-fuzzy logic to extract structured fields from messy pasted statement text. Extraction is probabilistic by nature, errors are silent, and the output is consumed downstream. Edge case: appears Lane-1-shaped but the logic posture pushes it to Lane 2.

files.snippet: Statement Importer

rationale: Extraction/parsing is YELLOW and probabilistic by nature — fuzzyMatch silently returns a best guess and errors are not surfaced, so it fails 5.4 (YELLOW requires 100% human review of every output, which a bulk CSV export does not provide). The output explicitly feeds a reconciliation workbook, which is an APPROVE trigger under STAR 3.1 (relied on for a reconciliation/control), so the tier is APPROVE — and the tier always wins, sending it to Lane 2 regardless of file count. That also fails 5.1 (tier is not USE). Bank-statement content is plausibly Client/Fund data feeding a reconciliation, reinforcing the escalation. This is the 'silently probabilistic parser' control: the classifier must not be fooled by the static single-page shape and must catch both the logic posture and the reconciliation reliance.

title: Single static fee-recalculation page that writes a client deliverable value (Approve-state, must be Lane 2)
description: A genuinely single static HTML file with deterministic GREEN arithmetic and no network calls — but it recomputes a management-fee figure that is copied directly into a client invoice and overwrites a cell in the shared fee register. Deterministic and static, yet the tier forces Lane 2. The marquee 'tier always wins' edge case.

files.snippet:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<title>Q2 Management Fee Recalc — Meridian Fund LP</title>
<!-- single static file, no external scripts, no fetch -->
<label>Committed capital ($) <input id="cap"></label>
<label>Fee rate (bps) <input id="bps"></label>
<output id="fee"></output>
<button id="save">Write to fee register</button>
<script>
  const calc = (cap, bps) => cap * (bps/10000); // deterministic, GREEN
  document.getElementById('save').onclick = () => {
    const fee = calc(+cap.value, +bps.value);
    // POST overwrites the authoritative fee register row
    fetch('/feeRegister/meridian-lp/q2', {method:'PUT', body: JSON.stringify({fee})});
    fee_out.value = 'Wrote $' + fee.toFixed(2) + ' to register';
  };
</script>

Used to recompute the Meridian Fund LP Q2 management fee.
The number produced here is pasted into the client's Q2 invoice and the
PUT updates the official fee register that finance reconciles against.

rationale: Logic is GREEN and the file is a single static page with inline script, which superficially screams Lane 1 — but STAR 3.1 puts it in APPROVE on three independent grounds: it touches Client/Fund data (Meridian Fund LP), its output is relied on for a client deliverable (the Q2 invoice) and a reconciliation (finance's fee register), and it WRITES to a source of truth (PUT /feeRegister). Per the playbook, THE TIER ALWAYS WINS: an Approve-state utility goes to Lane 2 even as a single static file, and additionally requires a Controlled Workflow Packet + AI Committee sign-off and Isolated Hosting. Failing 5.1 (not USE), 5.2 (Client/Fund data), and 5.3 (write to source of truth). This is the critical adversarial control proving determinism and small footprint never override the risk tier.

title: NAV reconciliation dashboard with live custodian feed and write-back
description: A Flask/React utility that authenticates to a custodian REST API, pulls live fund position data, reconciles it against the internal accounting system, and writes the matched/break results back into the recon database. Persists fund holdings in a local SQLite cache.

files.snippet:
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
import requests, sqlite3
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/api/recon/run', methods=['POST'])
def run_recon():
    token = get_custodian_token()  # OAuth client-credentials
    r = requests.get('https://api.statestreet-custody.com/v2/positions', headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'})
    positions = r.json()['holdings']
    db = sqlite3.connect('recon_cache.db')
    db.execute('INSERT INTO fund_positions (fund_id, cusip, qty, market_value) VALUES (?,?,?,?)', ...)
    db.commit()

def post_breaks(breaks):
    # writes reconciliation results to the system of record
    conn = pyodbc.connect(RECON_PROD_DSN)
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute("UPDATE recon.dbo.Breaks SET status=?, resolved_value=? WHERE break_id=?", ...)
    conn.commit()

const res = await fetch('/api/recon/run', { method: 'POST', credentials: 'include' });
const { breaks } = await res.json();

rationale: Touches Fund data and writes to a source of truth (the recon DB is a control/reconciliation system of record), so the tier is APPROVE (STAR 3.1) and the tier always wins -> Lane 2 / Isolated + AI Committee sign-off. It independently fails multiple Section 5 conditions: not USE tier (5.1), handles Fund data not General (5.2), writes to a production system (5.3), makes outbound calls to a non-allowlisted third-party custodian API (5.6), and persists Fund holdings locally (5.7). Logic is deterministic/GREEN, but that is irrelevant because the tier and several technical signals already force Lane 2.

title: Single-file client fee-schedule calculator relied on for invoicing
description: One self-contained static HTML file, no backend, no network calls, deterministic arithmetic only. It computes client management fees from a hardcoded fee schedule, and Finance pastes the output directly into client invoices, so the output is relied on for a client deliverable / financial reporting.

files.snippet:
<!DOCTYPE html><html><body>
<input id="aum" /> <input id="client" />
<script>
const SCHEDULE = { 'tier1': 0.0075, 'tier2': 0.0050 };
function calcFee(aum, tier){ return aum * SCHEDULE[tier]; } // deterministic, no fetch, no AI
document.getElementById('go').onclick = () => {
  out.textContent = '$' + calcFee(+aum.value, tierFor(+aum.value)).toFixed(2);
};
</script></body></html>
Finance uses the computed fee figure directly on the quarterly client invoice. Inputs include client name and AUM.

rationale: REVISED per 6/25 AI Architecture Group review: working ON client/fund data no longer forces a sign-off. This is a deterministic (GREEN) fee calculator with NO write to a system of record and no AI; finance reads and is responsible for the figure it places on the invoice. That is the canonical "scaling personal productivity tool" the meeting put on the light path (Lane 1). It escalates only if it writes to a system of record or its output is relied on without review.

title: Meeting-notes summarizer with direct api.anthropic.com call and client-side key
description: A static front-end utility for personal use that summarizes pasted meeting notes. It calls the Anthropic API directly from the browser using a key embedded in client-side JS, rather than the approved enterprise /api/chat proxy. General data, personal use, builder-reviewed.

files.snippet:
<textarea id="notes"></textarea>
<script type="module">
import Anthropic from 'https://esm.sh/@anthropic-ai/sdk';
const client = new Anthropic({ apiKey: 'sk-ant-api03-XXXX', dangerouslyAllowBrowser: true });
async function summarize(text){
  const m = await client.messages.create({ model: 'claude-opus-4-1', max_tokens: 500, messages:[{role:'user', content: 'Summarize: '+text}] });
  return m.content[0].text;
}
</script>

// direct provider endpoint, not the platform proxy
export const ANTHROPIC_BASE = 'https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages';

rationale: Tier is USE (personal, General data, builder-reviewed) and posture is YELLOW (summarizing). It fails 5.5 because it makes a runtime AI model call to a direct provider endpoint (api.anthropic.com) with a client-side key and an embedded SDK -- the policy explicitly states direct api.anthropic.com / SDK calls with client-side keys do NOT qualify; only the approved enterprise /api/chat proxy on General data would. It also fails 5.6: the import pulls a third-party SDK from an external CDN (esm.sh) and makes an outbound call outside any approved allowlist. Contrast: had this same summarizer routed through /api/chat with 100% human review of each summary, it could have stayed Lane 1. As written it is Lane 2 on a clear technical signal (direct runtime AI call) despite the low tier.

title: Capital-call PDF parser that is silently probabilistic
description: A Python utility that extracts amounts, dates, and investor names from capital-call notice PDFs using regex plus a layout/OCR heuristic, then emits a CSV the ops team consumes. No AI endpoint is called and no obvious model is present, but extraction is probabilistic by nature and errors are silent. Output is shared and relied on by the team.

files.snippet:
import pdfplumber, re

def extract(pdf_path):
    text = pdfplumber.open(pdf_path).pages[0].extract_text()
    amount = re.search(r'Call Amount[:\s]+\$([\d,\.]+)', text)  # best-effort match, no validation
    investor = re.search(r'Investor[:\s]+(.+)', text)
    due = guess_date(text)  # heuristic; falls back to 'nearest date-looking token'
    return { 'investor': investor.group(1) if investor else None, 'amount': amount and amount.group(1), 'due': due }

# ops imports this CSV into the cash-forecast workbook each morning
rows = [extract(p) for p in glob('inbox/*.pdf')]
write_csv('parsed_calls.csv', rows)  # no human verification step

rationale: This is the 'silently probabilistic parser' edge case. There is no runtime AI call and no obvious model, so 5.5/5.6 look fine -- but extraction/parsing is probabilistic by nature (Common Utility Patterns: Extraction/parsing -> Yellow -> Lane 2/Register, errors are silent). Posture is YELLOW and there is no 100% human review of every output before use, so it fails 5.4. The output is shared and relied on by the team and feeds a cash forecast, so the tier is at least REGISTER. Lesson for the classifier: absence of an AI keyword does not make a heuristic parser GREEN -- extraction relied upon downstream is YELLOW and routes to Lane 2.

title: Compliant /api/chat email drafter that tips to Lane 2 by persisting client data
description: An internal tool that drafts client correspondence. It correctly uses the approved enterprise /api/chat proxy (so the AI call itself is fine) and runs behind SSO, but it loads a grid widget from a third-party CDN and saves the generated client drafts plus account numbers to browser localStorage for reuse across sessions.

files.snippet:
<!-- approved proxy: this part is OK under 5.5 -->
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/some-grid-widget@latest/grid.min.js"></script>
<script>
async function draft(prompt){
  const r = await fetch('/api/chat', { method:'POST', credentials:'include', body: JSON.stringify({ message: prompt }) });
  return (await r.json()).reply;
}
</script>

rationale: Teaching case showing the /api/chat proxy alone does NOT decide the lane. The AI call routes through the approved enterprise proxy, so 5.5 is satisfied -- a tool using only that proxy on General data with human review could stay Lane 1. But this one tips into Lane 2 on other signals: it pulls a third-party script from an external CDN (jsdelivr) outside any allowlist, failing 5.6; and it persists client name + account numbers to localStorage, failing 5.7 (no persistence of sensitive data). Because it handles Client data and produces a client-facing deliverable (correspondence, disclosure applies), the tier is APPROVE, so 5.1 and 5.2 also fail and the tier forces Lane 2 / Isolated + AI Committee sign-off regardless. The classifier must not greenlight a tool just because it sees /api/chat.

title: Single static NAV reconciliation HTML page that writes a 'signed-off' flag to the accounting system of record
description: A self-contained single-file HTML/JS utility a fund accountant opens in the browser to reconcile a fund's striked NAV against the custodian feed. It loads Client/Fund holdings, computes the variance with deterministic arithmetic, and — on 'Approve' — POSTs a sign-off record back to the NAV book-of-record API. It is one static file with no build step, which is exactly why it is a deceptive Approve-state edge case: the tier wins over the single-static-file shape.

files.snippet: Daily NAV Reconciliation - Approve & Sign Off

rationale: Touches Client/Fund data (per-investor capital, holdings) and POSTs a 'SIGNED' sign-off to the NAV book-of-record (a source of truth) whose output is relied on for financial reporting and a reconciliation control -> Approve tier (STAR 3.1). Tier always wins, so it is Lane 2 even though it is a single deterministic static file. Fails 5.1 (tier is not USE), 5.2 (Client/Fund data), 5.3 (writes to a source of truth). Logic is GREEN, so 5.4 is not the trigger. Approve-state additionally needs a Controlled Workflow Packet + AI Committee sign-off (section 6).

title: Restricted-data invoice/contract parser that is silently probabilistic and feeds a billing reconciliation
description: A Python utility that ingests Restricted client fee schedules and PDF invoices, uses a regex+heuristic 'parser' that the builder believes is deterministic but is actually probabilistic (fuzzy matching, best-guess line-item extraction), and writes extracted fee amounts into a billing reconciliation workbook relied on for client invoicing. Edge case: a parser that looks rule-based but is silently probabilistic, with errors that are silent -> Lane 2.

files.snippet:

rationale: Handles Restricted data and produces output relied on for a billing reconciliation / client invoicing -> Approve tier. The 'parser' is YELLOW: fuzzy matching (rapidfuzz) and best-guess extraction make it silently probabilistic with errors that are not surfaced — exactly the extraction/parsing pattern the policy calls Lane 2. Writes into a source-of-truth reconciliation workbook. Fails 5.1 (not USE), 5.2 (Restricted data), 5.3 (writes to source of truth), and 5.4 (YELLOW logic without 100% human review of every output before use). Approve-state -> Lane 2 + Controlled Workflow Packet + AI Committee.

title: Compliance breach-monitoring tool calling api.anthropic.com directly with a client-side key on Restricted holdings
description: A Node/React utility that screens Restricted fund holdings against investment-mandate / compliance limits and produces a breach report a compliance officer relies on as a control. It calls the Anthropic API directly with a hard-coded client-side key to 'explain' borderline breaches, and persists the flagged Restricted holdings to local storage. Edge case stacking multiple Lane-1 failures plus the direct-model-call disqualifier.
rationale: Output is a compliance breach report relied on as a control over Restricted holdings -> Approve tier; tier wins regardless. Direct api.anthropic.com call via the embedded SDK with a client-side key explicitly does NOT qualify under 5.5. The model-generated breach explanation is YELLOW judgment relied on without guaranteed human review (5.4). Restricted data (5.2), persisted to local storage (5.7), and tier is not USE (5.1). No write-to-source-of-truth in the snippet, so 5.3 is not listed. Approve-state -> Lane 2 + Committee.

title: Wire/payment instruction generator that writes to the treasury source of truth — deterministic but still Approve
description: A fully deterministic Python service that builds outbound wire payment instructions for fund capital calls/distributions from Client/Fund subscription data and submits them to the treasury/payments system of record. No AI anywhere, GREEN logic, clean code — the hard case is that a perfectly deterministic, non-AI tool is STILL Approve-state because it writes payment instructions to a source of truth relied on for client money movement.

files.snippet:
import requests
def submit(wires):
    # writes payment instructions to the treasury system of record
    r = requests.post('https://treasury.gpfundsolutions.internal/api/v2/payment-batches',
                      json={'batch': wires, 'status': 'RELEASE'}, headers=auth())
    r.raise_for_status()  # source of truth for client money movement
    return r.json()

rationale: Generates and releases wire payment instructions written to the treasury system of record using Client/Fund + Restricted banking data — money movement relied on for a client deliverable -> Approve tier. Logic is GREEN and there is no AI at all, so 5.4 and 5.5 pass cleanly, demonstrating that determinism does not rescue an Approve-state utility: the tier always wins. Fails 5.1 (not USE), 5.2 (Client/Fund + Restricted data), 5.3 (writes to source of truth). Approve-state -> Lane 2 / Isolated + AI Committee sign-off (section 6).

title: Investor-facing capital account statement generator using the approved /api/chat proxy — still Approve because output is a client deliverable
description: A React tool that drafts narrative capital-account statements sent to LPs. It correctly uses ONLY the approved enterprise /api/chat proxy (no direct model SDK, no client-side key) and runs behind SSO — so it passes the 5.5/5.6 traps that catch lesser tools. The catch is the tier: it pulls Client/Fund capital-account figures and the drafted narrative is a client-facing deliverable, which is Approve-state. This is the 'looks like it could be Lane 1 / approved proxy' edge case that the tier overrides.
rationale: The runtime AI call uses the approved /api/chat enterprise proxy (5.5 PASS) and runs behind SSO with no third-party scripts (5.6 PASS) — the traps that would catch a careless tool are avoided. But it processes Client/Fund capital-account data and produces an investor-facing capital account statement, a client deliverable -> Approve tier; tier wins. The drafting is YELLOW/Red (probabilistic, client-facing, disclosure applies) and is not guaranteed 100% human-reviewed (5.4). Fails 5.1 (not USE) and 5.2 (Client/Fund data). No write-to-source-of-truth shown, so 5.3 is not listed. Approve-state -> Lane 2 + Controlled Workflow Packet + AI Committee.

title: Approved /api/chat proxy tool — clean Lane 1 (the near-miss that PASSES)
description: A single-page internal helper that rewrites a user's rough meeting note into tidy bullet points by POSTing to the approved enterprise platform proxy /api/chat. It runs behind GPFS SSO, makes no other outbound calls, persists nothing, and operates only on General data the builder pastes in. This is the adversarial 'AI call' case that the analyzer must NOT flag — it hits the explicit 5.5 carve-out for the approved enterprise endpoint on General data, and every output is shown to the builder for review.
rationale: The only AI call is to the approved platform proxy /api/chat on General data — the explicit exception in 5.5, so 5.5 is satisfied, not failed. No direct api.anthropic.com/api.openai.com call, no client-side key. Tier is USE (personal, General data, builder-reviewed), satisfying 5.1/5.2. No writes (5.3). Logic is YELLOW (rewrite is probabilistic) but the builder reviews every output before use, satisfying 5.4. Same-origin fetch, no third-party <script src>, runs behind SSO (5.6). Nothing persisted (5.7). This is the trap: an 'AI tool' that legitimately stays Lane 1 because it uses the sanctioned proxy.

title: Direct api.anthropic.com call with client-side key disguised as a proxy
description: Almost identical UX to EDGE-01 — same 'tidy my notes' purpose, same General data — but instead of the approved proxy it calls api.anthropic.com directly from the browser using a key embedded in the front-end code. The function is even named callProxy() to look benign. This is the inverse near-miss: it must fail 5.5 (direct provider call with client-side key) and also 5.6 (un-allowlisted outbound network call).
rationale: Tier stays USE (General data, personal note-tidying), so the tier does not force Lane 2 — but it still fails Lane 1 on conditions. 5.5 fails because this is a DIRECT call to api.anthropic.com with a client-side key, which the policy explicitly says does NOT qualify for the enterprise-endpoint exception. 5.6 also fails: api.anthropic.com is an un-allowlisted outbound call. The misleadingly-named callProxy() and matching purpose make this the perfect adversarial twin to EDGE-01; the analyzer must read the URL/host and key, not the function name.

title: Deterministic currency formatter — clean Lane 1 (looks risky, isn't)
description: A pure formatting widget: paste a column of raw numbers, get them rendered as USD strings with thousands separators and two decimals for pasting into a deck. It mentions 'amount', 'currency', and 'financial' in its labels and even has a string that says 'reconciliation-ready format', which can bait a keyword classifier toward Approve. But it changes no numeric values, writes nowhere, calls no model, persists nothing — it is a textbook GREEN deterministic formatter that stays Lane 1.

files.snippet:
Currency Formatter — Utility — not production-hardened

Paste raw amounts; get reconciliation-ready display strings. Values are not altered, only formatted.

rationale: Per the COMMON UTILITY PATTERNS table, data formatting is GREEN/Lane 1 and escalates only if it changes numeric values used downstream — here it only changes the display representation (Intl.NumberFormat), not the underlying values, and writes nowhere. Tier is USE: General-data display strings, builder-reviewed, no client/fund data and no source-of-truth write, so it never reaches Approve. Logic is GREEN deterministic (5.4 met), no AI call (5.5 met), no network/third-party script (5.6 met), no persistence (5.7 met). The 'reconciliation-ready' and 'currency' wording is the adversarial bait that should NOT raise the tier.

title: Silently-probabilistic invoice parser branded as 'validation' — Register/Lane 2
description: A tool the builder calls a 'rules-based validator' but which actually parses unstructured vendor invoice text into structured fields (vendor, amount, due date) using fuzzy regex heuristics and a confidence score, then is shared with the AP team who rely on the extracted figures. The output is silently wrong on edge cases. This is the deterministic-formatter look-alike that is actually probabilistic extraction relied on by others — it must land Lane 2 / Register on tier and on 5.4.

files.snippet:
# 'Invoice validator' — shared with AP team, used to pre-fill the payment sheet
import re

def parse_invoice(text):
    amt = re.search(r'(?:total|amount due)[^0-9]{0,20}([0-9,]+\.\d{2})', text, re.I)
    vendor = re.search(r'(?:from|vendor)[:\s]+([A-Z][A-Za-z0-9 &.,-]{2,40})', text)
    due = re.search(r'(?:due)[^0-9]{0,15}(\d{1,2}[/-]\d{1,2}[/-]\d{2,4})', text, re.I)
    # heuristic confidence — errors are silent when a field is missed or mis-matched
    fields = [amt, vendor, due]
    confidence = sum(1 for f in fields if f) / len(fields)
    return {
        'vendor': vendor.group(1) if vendor else None,
        'amount': amt.group(1) if amt else None,
        'due_date': due.group(1) if due else None,
        'confidence': confidence
    }

# Invoice Validator
Drop in raw invoice text; it extracts vendor / amount / due date so the AP team can paste straight into the weekly payment run. Shared on the team drive. "Rules-based, no AI."

rationale: Despite the 'rules-based validator, no AI' framing, this is extraction/parsing of unstructured text — per the patterns table that is YELLOW and Lane 2/Register because errors are silent. It is shared and relied on by the AP team (not just the builder), so the tier is at least REGISTER, failing 5.1 (Lane 1 needs USE or an approved Pilot). Logic posture is effectively YELLOW/probabilistic via fuzzy regex + confidence score, and there is no 100% human review claim, so 5.4 fails. No live model call so 5.5 is not the trigger — the failure is the silently-probabilistic-and-relied-upon nature, the exact deterministic-vs-probabilistic trap. (It stops short of Approve because amounts feed a manual payment run rather than being written to a source of truth.)

title: Single static file that caches Client/Fund data in localStorage — Approve-state forced to Lane 2
description: One self-contained HTML file, no backend, no AI, no outbound calls — superficially the simplest possible Lane 1 candidate. But it lets a user paste a fund's investor allocation table and caches it in browser localStorage between sessions, and its formatted output is used to feed a client capital-call statement. Two things force it out of Lane 1: it touches Client/Fund data used for a client deliverable (Approve tier — and the tier always wins), and it persists sensitive data in localStorage. The 'single static file' framing is the trap: simplicity does not buy Lane 1.

files.snippet: Capital Call Allocation Helper
Used by the fund accounting team to lay out LP commitments/called amounts for the quarterly capital call statement sent to investors. Output gets copied into the client-facing statement template.

rationale: Tier is APPROVE: it handles Client/Fund (LP allocation) data AND its output is relied on for a client deliverable (the investor capital-call statement) — per STAR 3.1 either trigger alone is Approve. THE TIER ALWAYS WINS, so even though it is a single static file with no AI and no network calls, it goes to Lane 2 (Approve-state requires a Controlled Workflow Packet + AI Committee). Mapped to Lane-1 conditions it also concretely fails 5.1 (tier not USE), 5.2 (Client/Fund data, not General), and 5.7 (persists sensitive fund/investor data in localStorage). This is the headline edge case: maximal structural simplicity, maximal tier — Lane 2 regardless.

title: Commented-out AI call must not flip the lane
description: A deterministic currency formatter with an old AI call left in as a comment.
rationale: The only AI reference is inside a comment; comment-aware scanning means it does not count. Deterministic, general data, no writes -> Lane 1 (light).

title: AI SDK listed in package.json but never imported
description: A static app that lists @anthropic-ai/sdk as a dependency but never imports or calls it.

title: Drizzle ORM update + relative PUT writing back to the investor capital-account ledger
description: A POC that recomputes an LP's drawn commitment after a capital call and persists the new figure back to the fund's capital-account ledger. The persistence is a Drizzle ORM mutation (db.update(capitalAccounts).set(...).where(...)) plus a sync to the fund-admin system via a relative-path request whose verb is assembled as ['P','U','T'].join('') and passed under a 'httpMethod' key. The engine's write detectors all miss it, so it looks like a read-only formatter and lands Lane 1; the correct answer is Approve-state because it updates a system of record holding client/fund data.
rationale: The code writes to a system of record (5.3) — an investor capital-account ledger holding client/fund data — which forces Approve-state per the tier-wins rule (and contradicts 5.2 General-data-only since the write is authoritative). Drizzle's chained .update(table).set(...) is not literal 'UPDATE <word> SET' SQL, and neither 'drizzle-orm' nor 'postgres-js' is in DB_ORM_WRITE's driver list, so the ORM write is invisible. The sync is a relative path (no https://, so MUTATING_EXTERNAL/MUTATING_FETCH_EXTERNAL miss) with the verb computed and stored under 'httpMethod' rather than the literal 'method:' key METHOD_WRITE looks for. With zero write evidence the engine sets writeAuthority='none' and reports a read-only Lane 1 formatter; the true governance answer is Approve-state -> Lane 2 with AI Committee sign-off.

title: Direct OpenAI call via fragment-assembled host and dynamic import(), summarizing investor statements
description: An IR helper that drafts a plain-English summary of an LP's capital-account statement by calling OpenAI's chat-completions endpoint directly. The vendor host is assembled as 'op'+'enai' then 'https://api.'+SVC+'.com/...', and the SDK is pulled in with await import(SVC) where SVC is a runtime variable — so no literal 'api.openai.com', no quoted 'openai', and no baseURL: key ever appears in source. The engine sees no runtime AI call and clears 5.5, reporting Lane 1; the correct answer is Lane 2 because a direct external AI model call (not the approved proxy) disqualifies Lane 1.
rationale: This is a direct runtime call to a public LLM vendor (OpenAI) outside the approved same-origin /api/chat proxy, which fails 5.5 and forces Lane 2 regardless of tier. Every detector misses: the host never appears as the literal 'api.openai.com' (it is 'op'+'enai' then concatenated), so VENDOR_HOST and all direct-host patterns are clean; the SDK arrives via await import(SVC) on a variable specifier, evading all literal-package SDK patterns and the quoted 'openai' token; the endpoint is assigned to ENDPOINT rather than a baseURL: key, dodging the baseURL pattern; and the fetch target is a variable, so the approved-proxy patterns don't fire either. The engine therefore computes directAI=false and passes 5.5, wrongly clearing it for Lane 1. (It is also probabilistic drafting over investor data, reinforcing Lane 2; note the tier stays Use because no write/deliverable/unreviewed-probabilistic-over-restricted trigger is asserted, but 5.5 alone is dispositive for Lane 2.)

title: Deterministic allocation-percentage display tool, personal use (escalated to Approve)
description: A single static page that takes pasted general allocation weights and renders an on-screen HTML table of percentages. Pure arithmetic plus toFixed formatting; no network, no storage, no AI, no write. The only 'export' is window.print(). The builder uses it personally for scratch notes. Despite being textbook Green/Use/Lane-1, the function name generateAllocationReport matches STRONG_RELIANCE_EXPORT (generate\w*(report...)) so auto-reliance becomes 'deliverable' and the tier jumps to Approve; the same substring matches the probabilistic-summarize-draft pattern (generate\w*Report), flipping posture to Yellow. The engine returns Lane 2 (Approve-state), driven by §5.1 risk tier.

files.snippet:
Target vs Actual Allocation

Paste sample weights, see the split. Nothing leaves the page.

# Allocation Report
Personal scratch tool. Paste any sample allocation weights and eyeball the
percentage split on screen. Runs entirely in the browser — no data leaves the
page, nothing is saved, no server. General/demo data only. Built by me, for me.

rationale: Per policy this is the textbook clean Lane 1 case: logic is GREEN (deterministic arithmetic + Intl-style formatting, no parsing/judgment), it only reads and surfaces general sample data, there is no write to any system of record, no runtime AI call, no third-party scripts or outbound calls, and no persistence. The output is shown on screen for the builder, who uses it personally — no one relies on it as a client deliverable, so the tier is USE, not Approve. The engine over-escalates purely on the lexical coincidence that 'generateAllocationReport' contains 'Report': STRONG_RELIANCE_EXPORT infers reliance='deliverable' (Approve) and the probabilistic-summarize-draft pattern marks posture Yellow, even though the function returns a plain percentage array and the only 'export' is window.print(). Reliance is explicitly an un-inferable, human-confirmed dimension; defaulting a name match to 'deliverable' is exactly the over-escalation the policy warns against.

title: Tab UI that remembers theme/last-tab in localStorage (escalated to Lane 2 on §5.7)
description: A static client-side tab switcher over a pasted general JSON array. It saves the last-selected tab and the light/dark theme to localStorage so the view is restored on reload — plainly non-sensitive UI preference state. There is no network, no AI, no write to any record, and no actual client/fund data. But the words 'portfolio' and 'holdings' occur as a tab enum (PORTFOLIO:'portfolio'), as button text, and in data-name attributes. detectRestricted's WEAK_SCOPE matches them; SCOPE_BENIGN only neutralizes CSS-selector / class= / 'name:'{ forms, so these non-CSS occurrences slip through and force dataScope='restricted'. Combined with the localStorage calls, §5.7 (no local persistence of restricted data) flips to fail and the engine returns Lane 2.

files.snippet:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head><meta charset="utf-8"><title>Tabs</title>
<style>.tab{padding:.5rem 1rem}.tab.active{font-weight:700}</style>
</head>
<body data-theme="light">
  <nav>
    <button class="tab" data-name="overview">Overview</button>
    <button class="tab" data-name="portfolio">Portfolio</button>
    <button class="tab" data-name="holdings">Holdings</button>
  </nav>
  <main id="view"></main>
  <script src="./app.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

rationale: This is a clean Lane 1 utility: deterministic (GREEN) logic, general data only, no write to a system of record, no runtime AI, no third-party scripts/outbound calls. It persists only UI chrome — the last-open tab and the theme — which §5.7 permits because that is general data, not Client/Fund/Restricted. The words 'portfolio' and 'holdings' here are interface labels and an enum, never actual data, so the real data scope is general and §5.7 holds. The engine over-escalates because detectRestricted treats any WEAK_SCOPE term outside a stylesheet as restricted data unless SCOPE_BENIGN matches, and SCOPE_BENIGN only covers CSS-selector / class-attribute / 'identifier:'-or-'{' forms — it does not cover a quoted enum value, button text, or a data-name attribute. The mis-inferred 'restricted' scope then combines with the harmless localStorage calls to fail §5.7, bouncing a Lane 1 tool to Lane 2. Data sensitivity is an un-inferable dimension the policy says to confirm with the user, not to assume from interface vocabulary.

title: Local risk scorer over fund holdings with explicit per-item human review (stays Lane 1)
description: A single React component runs a local probabilistic risk scorer over fund holdings and renders one suggestion per row. Each row has an explicit Approve control the human must click before the value is used; nothing is batched, exported, or written. This sits exactly on the §5.4 line: probabilistic output on Client/Fund data is acceptable for Lane 1 only when 100% of outputs are reviewed every time. The mirror image (swap the per-row Approve for an exportCsv(rows) of the same scores) flips to Approve, so this case verifies the engine reads the review condition correctly rather than escalating on restricted+probabilistic alone.
rationale: STRONG_ENTITY fires (fund name/NAV, fundId) so dataScope auto-resolves to restricted, and predictRisk(...)/confidence make logic probabilistic (Yellow). But there is no SILENT_BATCH token — every suggestion is shown in a row with an explicit Approve/Override action, so humanReview is effectively yes and reliedProbabilistic is false. With no write, no deliverable export, and no AI/CDN/persistence, the tier stays Use and all of §5.2–§5.7 hold: restricted data that is only read/formatted/surfaced and reviewed every time stays Lane 1. The policy is explicit that merely touching Client/Fund data is not enough to escalate. This is the clean side of the per-item-review boundary; the same code exporting the scores instead would correctly become Approve.

title: Same-origin /api/chat that a backend forwards to a vendor with a server-side key (should be Lane 2)
description: The front end calls the relative path /api/chat, which matches the approved enterprise-proxy shape (proxyAI, the one Lane-1-permitted AI call). But the repo also ships server/chat_proxy.py: a plain handler that POSTs the prompt to an env-configured upstream LLM endpoint using a server-side API key. The vendor host is hidden behind os.environ (so no vendor-host literal and no SDK import to detect), the handler uses no web framework, and the file path is not under pages/api, app/api, or functions/*.js — so the engine's BACKEND_PATH safety net misses too. This sits exactly on the boundary the playbook calls out: a same-origin /api/chat backed by the platform's keyless proxy is Lane 1, but the same shape forwarding to a vendor with a server key is effectively a direct AI integration and needs a backend — Lane 2.

files.snippet:
import os, requests

# Deployed alongside the static front end. /api/chat is NOT the platform proxy:
# this handler forwards the prompt to our own LLM vendor account using a
# server-side key. The upstream host is supplied at deploy time via env so it
# never appears as a literal in source.
UPSTREAM = os.environ["LLM_UPSTREAM_URL"]
API_KEY = os.environ["LLM_API_KEY"]

def handle_chat(event):
    payload = {"model": os.environ.get("LLM_MODEL", "gpt-4o"),
               "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": event["prompt"]}]}
    r = requests.post(UPSTREAM, json=payload,
                      headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"})
    return {"reply": r.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]}

rationale: Governance-correct answer is Lane 2: the relative /api/chat is not the approved keyless platform proxy — it is backed by an app-owned server that forwards to a non-approved LLM vendor with a server-side key. The playbook treats a same-origin /api/chat that the app's own backend forwards to a vendor as effectively direct (fails §5.5) and as needing a backend (§6 backend-needed). It is Use tier on general chat data, not Approve, so the only failing Lane-1 condition is §5.5 (plus the backend/host fact). This targets the proxy-spoof edge: the engine resolves proxyAI=true and, because the forwarder hides the vendor host behind os.environ, uses no framework, and lives at server/chat_proxy.py (outside the BACKEND_PATH allow-shapes), neither directAI nor backend fires — so it risks calling this Lane 1 when the correct lane is Lane 2.

title: Go/Gin capital-call service writes to the investor ledger and custodian — engine sees nothing
description: A two-file Go (Gin + GORM) backend POC. An HTTP handler registers a capital call into the investor ledger database (db.Create / db.Update on investor status) and then PUTs the redemption to the custodian's authoritative position record via http.NewRequest("PUT", ...). It reads/writes investor, fund, and NAV data. There is no raw SQL, no JS ORM method, no fetch/axios, no Dockerfile — so every write- and backend-detector in the engine stays silent. Verified against the live engine: it returns Lane 1 (light), high confidence, with §5.3 (no writes) and §6 (self-contained front-end) both PASSING.
rationale: Per policy this is Approve-state: it UPDATES a system of record (the investor ledger via gorm db.Create/db.Update, plus an authoritative PUT to the custodian) over investor/fund/NAV data — the exact 'updates a system of record' Approve trigger. It is also unambiguously Lane 2 on §6 (a real Gin server, not a static file) and §5.3 (writes). The engine instead returns Lane 1 with every §5 passing because its write detectors only know SQL keywords, specific JS ORM methods, and fetch/axios+method, and its backend detectors only know express/flask/fastapi/app.listen/JS paths — none of which match Go's gorm methods, http.NewRequest, or r.Run/gin. With no Dockerfile to catch via BACKEND_PATH, the whole backend and its writes are invisible. High confidence makes the wrong verdict especially dangerous.

title: Python `from openai import OpenAI` direct vendor call passes §5.5 as 'no AI calls'
description: A single-file Python CLI helper that tags public press headlines by sentiment using a DIRECT OpenAI vendor call (from openai import OpenAI; gw = OpenAI(base_url=...); gw.chat.completions.create(...)). It is otherwise a clean Lane-1 candidate: read-only, personal, general/public data, output printed for a human to read, nothing stored, no backend server, no third-party CDN. The only thing that should knock it off Lane 1 is the direct vendor AI call, which Lane 1 forbids (only the same-origin /api/chat proxy is allowed). Verified against the live engine: it returns Lane 1 (light), HIGH confidence, and §5.5 explicitly reads 'Makes no runtime AI model calls.'
rationale: Per policy this is Lane 2: it makes a DIRECT external AI model call (the OpenAI SDK to a vendor, not the approved same-origin /api/chat proxy), which fails §5.5 — the explicit 'no direct external AI call' Lane-1 condition. It is NOT Approve, because it is read-only (no write to a system of record), personal, on general/public data, and every output is shown to a human — so no Approve trigger applies; tier stays Use. The engine wrongly lands Lane 1 because all three §5.5 detectors are JS-shaped: VENDOR_HOST needs a literal host (here base_url is an env var), the SDK patterns need a quoted 'openai' or `new OpenAI(` (Python uses bare import and OpenAI() with no `new`), and the key detector needs a key literal or VITE_/NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix (here it reads OAI_GATEWAY). The result is the most dangerous failure mode: a confident, green Lane-1 verdict that hands an ungoverned direct-LLM call to shared hosting.

title: Investor allocation saver — README says read-only, code POSTs writes to a relative /save path
description: A multi-file IR tool whose README insists it is 'read-only, no data written back, no AI.' The GET that loads LP capital-account/NAV figures is genuinely read-only, but the Save button handler does fetch('/api/allocations/save', { method: 'POST', ... }) to record the allocation note back to the allocations service. Because the call is same-origin (relative path) and uses POST, the engine's write detectors all miss it: MUTATING_FETCH_EXTERNAL requires an absolute https:// host, MUTATING_EXTERNAL/XHR_MUTATE_EXTERNAL require an external host, and METHOD_WRITE only fires on PUT/PATCH/DELETE. The engine returns Lane 1 (light) with HIGH confidence and even lists the save-fetch as benign evidence under c55. Verified: making the identical call external (Control 1) or PUT (Control 2) correctly flips it to Approve, proving the relative-POST blind spot.

files.snippet:
# Allocation Note Saver

A lightweight, **read-only** helper for the investor relations team. It pulls each

you eyeball it. **No data is written back** — it only reads and surfaces. No AI.
Deterministic formatting only. Safe to host as-is.
Investor Allocation Notes

rationale: The handler POSTs the note back to /api/allocations/save — a write to a system of record (investor allocations), which §5.3 forbids for Lane 1, and it does so over restricted fund data (capital account, NAV, investor name), so §5.2's read-and-format-only carve-out does not apply. An authoritative write forces tier = Approve (§5.1, the tier always wins). Engine actual: Lane 1 (light), HIGH confidence — c53 wrongly passes because the write is a same-origin POST to a relative write-y path, which sits in the exact gap between MUTATING_FETCH_EXTERNAL (external-only) and METHOD_WRITE (PUT/PATCH/DELETE-only).

title: Meeting-note tidier — OpenAI SDK + client-side key hidden in a module named summarize.spec.js
description: A monorepo whose README and app loudly claim deterministic, no-AI cleanup. lib/clean.js really is deterministic. But app.js also imports summarize() from lib/summarize.spec.js — named as the summarizer 'specification', not a test — and calls it on every click. That module imports the OpenAI SDK, instantiates it with import.meta.env.VITE_OPENAI_API_KEY and dangerouslyAllowBrowser: true, and calls chat.completions.create. Because fileRole classifies *.spec.js as test role, every runtime-scoped signal (runtime-ai-vendor-sdk-import, client-side-model-api-key, and even outbound) is marked non-runtime, so firedRuntime is false and directAI never fires. Verified: engine returns Lane 1 (light) with HIGH confidence (c55 passes) — confidence stays high because index.html/app.js/clean.js are real runtime source, so 'minified/no-source' downgrades don't kick in.

rationale: lib/summarize.spec.js is imported by app.js and invoked on every click — it is shipped runtime code, not a test. It makes a direct external AI call via the OpenAI SDK with a client-side key (dangerouslyAllowBrowser: true), which §5.5 disqualifies from Lane 1, and the summarizing output is probabilistic YELLOW logic under §5.4. Data is general and nothing is shared, so tier stays Use, but any single Lane-1 failure means Lane 2. Engine actual: Lane 1 (light), HIGH confidence — fileRole() classifies *.spec.js as test role, so the runtime-scoped AI/key/outbound signals are marked non-runtime and directAI never fires; c55 wrongly passes. A .spec.js suffix used as 'specification' rather than 'test' silently demotes the disqualifier.

title: Excel VBA macro summarizing deal notes via direct OpenAI call
description: An Excel VBA macro a fund accountant runs from a workbook. It reads a range of rough deal notes (column B), POSTs each note to https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions through MSXML2.XMLHTTP60 with a hard-coded client-side API key, parses the one-line summary out of the JSON response, and writes it back into the adjacent column C of the same sheet. The notes are general-ish internal text, but the running utility makes a DIRECT vendor AI call (not the approved enterprise /api/chat proxy) using an embedded API key, and writes the probabilistic summaries back in bulk with no per-row human review. Probes whether the engine catches a non-JS/non-Python runtime AI call where the model host is a string concatenated into the URL and the SDK is the raw WinHTTP/MSXML stack rather than an npm/pip package.
attack: Direct vendor AI call hidden in a non-web language (VBA/MSXML) with no AI SDK import and a bulk-write of unreviewed probabilistic output.

files.snippet:
Attribute VB_Name = "modSummarizeDealNotes"
Option Explicit

' Run from the "Deal Notes" tab. Reads rough notes in column B,
' asks OpenAI for a one-line summary, writes it to column C.
Private Const OPENAI_URL As String = "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions"
Private Const OPENAI_KEY As String = "sk-proj-7Qd3xZ0fA1mB9kLpR2sT4uVwX6yZ8a0"

Public Sub SummarizeDealNotes()
    Dim ws As Worksheet, lastRow As Long, r As Long
    Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Deal Notes")
    lastRow = ws.Cells(ws.Rows.Count, "B").End(xlUp).Row

    Application.ScreenUpdating = False
    For r = 2 To lastRow
        Dim note As String
        note = Trim$(ws.Cells(r, "B").Value)
        If Len(note) > 0 Then
            ws.Cells(r, "C").Value = GetSummary(note)
        End If
    Next r
    Application.ScreenUpdating = True

    MsgBox "Summarized " & (lastRow - 1) & " deal notes.", vbInformation
End Sub

Private Function GetSummary(ByVal note As String) As String
    Dim http As Object, body As String, resp As String
    Set http = CreateObject("MSXML2.XMLHTTP.6.0")

    body = "{""model"":""gpt-4o-mini"",""messages"":[" & _
           "{""role"":""system"",""content"":""Summarize the deal note in one line.""}," & _
           "{""role"":""user"",""content"":""" & JsonEscape(note) & """}]}"

    http.Open "POST", OPENAI_URL, False
    http.setRequestHeader "Content-Type", "application/json"
    http.setRequestHeader "Authorization", "Bearer " & OPENAI_KEY
    http.send body

    resp = http.responseText
    GetSummary = ExtractContent(resp)
End Function

Private Function JsonEscape(ByVal s As String) As String
    s = Replace(s, "\", "\\")
    s = Replace(s, """", "\""")
    JsonEscape = Replace(Replace(s, vbCr, " "), vbLf, " ")
End Function

Private Function ExtractContent(ByVal resp As String) As String
    Dim p As Long, q As Long
    p = InStr(resp, """content"":""")
    If p = 0 Then ExtractContent = "": Exit Function
    p = p + Len("""content"":""")
    q = InStr(p, resp, """")
    ExtractContent = Replace(Mid$(resp, p, q - p), "\n", " ")
End Function

rationale: Lane 2, and the AI gate is the decisive blocker. 5.5 fails outright: the running macro makes a DIRECT runtime call to api.openai.com (OPENAI_URL concatenated into http.Open "POST") with a Bearer key (Authorization header), which is a direct vendor host, NOT the approved same-origin /api/chat enterprise proxy — a hard FAIL regardless of how benign the notes are. 5.4 also fails: the output is probabilistic (a model-generated summary, model:gpt-4o-mini + messages[role,content]) and is written back to column C in a bulk For loop with no per-row human review before use, so the Yellow-with-100%-review carve-out does not apply. 5.6 fails too: api.openai.com is an un-allowlisted outbound call. On tier (Step 1): it is at least Register (a shared, repeatable accountant tool relied on by others), and arguably Approve-state because it relies on probabilistic output over fund deal data without a human checking every output (Step 1c) — but tier is moot here since any single 5.x failure forces Lane 2. The attack value: there is NO AI SDK import (no openai npm/pip package), the model host arrives only as a String constant, and the transport is raw MSXML2.XMLHTTP rather than fetch/requests — so a naive scanner keying on SDK imports or fetch('https://api.openai.com') would miss it; the AI_PAYLOAD fingerprint (model: + messages[role,content]) plus the api.openai.com URL string must fire to catch it. Note the JSON contains role/content keys (chat payload, NOT a permissions/sharing signal) and the POST is to an AI endpoint (NOT a 5.3 source-of-truth write).

title: Scheduled PowerShell expense categorizer (direct OpenAI call, batch export to shared drive)
description: A PowerShell maintenance script run on a schedule on an analyst workstation. It reads expense rows from a CSV, sends each expense to https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions via Invoke-RestMethod authenticated with $env:OPENAI_API_KEY to assign a GL category, then writes the categorized results to a shared finance network drive. The categorization is probabilistic, performed in batch, and exported with no per-row human review. Probes whether the engine catches a direct vendor AI host call (5.5), unreviewed probabilistic output feeding a finance deliverable (5.4 / tier (b)/(c)), and a write to a shared authoritative location.
attack: Direct vendor AI host (api.openai.com) inside a benign-looking scheduled .ps1 that batch-exports unreviewed model output to a shared finance drive — engine may under-read a "just categorizing a CSV" script.
rationale: Tier is Approve-state under STEP 1: the script relies on probabilistic AI output (api.openai.com chat completion) over fund/firm financial data with NO human reviewing each row (condition (c)), and that output feeds a finance deliverable used by others — the categorized expense file on the shared drive (condition (b)). The Approve tier alone forces Lane 2 (fails 5.1). It additionally fails multiple §5 conditions: 5.5 — it makes a runtime AI model call to a direct vendor host (https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions) with a client-side key ($env:OPENAI_API_KEY), not the approved same-origin /api/chat proxy; 5.4 — logic is probabilistic (Yellow) and exported in batch with zero per-row human review, which the policy explicitly says fails; 5.6 — un-allowlisted outbound network call to api.openai.com. It is also not a hostable web utility: it is a scheduled local script writing to a shared network path (\\fileserver\finance\expense_cat.csv), a relied-on shared artifact (Register-or-higher sharing) and Section 6 backend/scheduled-runtime territory. Any one of 5.1, 5.4, 5.5 is independently dispositive; together they make this unambiguously Lane 2 / Approve.

title: R portfolio risk-band classifier: Anthropic API + warehouse write-back
description: An R batch script for the portfolio team that pulls investor positions into a data frame, calls Anthropic's api.anthropic.com/v1/messages directly (via httr::POST) to assign each position a risk band, then appends the AI-classified table back into the SQL Server warehouse with DBI::dbWriteTable(append=TRUE). It probes whether the engine catches a direct vendor AI host call inside an httr verb, an authoritative DB write through DBI (not an ORM), and the fact that the probabilistic risk bands are written unreviewed. All three are independent Lane-2 / Approve forcers.
attack: Vendor AI host buried in an httr::POST string-built body, plus a DBI::dbWriteTable warehouse write that isn't an obvious ORM .save()/persist call, plus unreviewed probabilistic output piped straight into a system of record.

files.snippet:
# classify_investor_risk_bands.R
# Portfolio team: classify each investor position into a risk band and
# load the result back into the EDW. Run nightly via the RStudio Connect scheduler.
library(DBI)
library(odbc)
library(httr)
library(jsonlite)
library(dplyr)

ANTHROPIC_KEY <- Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")

con <- dbConnect(
  odbc::odbc(),
  Driver   = "ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server",
  Server   = "gpfs-edw-prod.database.windows.net",
  Database = "PortfolioWarehouse",
  Authentication = "ActiveDirectoryIntegrated"
)

positions <- dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT investor_id, fund_code, security, market_value, currency
  FROM dbo.investor_positions
  WHERE as_of_date = CAST(GETDATE() AS date)
")

classify_band <- function(row) {
  prompt <- sprintf(
    "Investor %s holds %s (%s %.2f) in fund %s. Reply with one risk band: LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH.",
    row$investor_id, row$security, row$currency, row$market_value, row$fund_code
  )
  resp <- POST(
    url = "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
    add_headers(
      "x-api-key"         = ANTHROPIC_KEY,
      "anthropic-version" = "2023-06-01",
      "content-type"      = "application/json"
    ),
    body = toJSON(list(
      model      = "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
      max_tokens = 8,
      messages   = list(list(role = "user", content = prompt))
    ), auto_unbox = TRUE),
    encode = "raw"
  )
  stop_for_status(resp)
  trimws(content(resp)$content[[1]]$text)
}

positions$risk_band <- vapply(
  split(positions, seq_len(nrow(positions))),
  classify_band,
  character(1)
)

out <- positions %>%
  transmute(investor_id, fund_code, security, risk_band,
            scored_at = Sys.time())

dbWriteTable(con, "investor_risk_bands", out, append = TRUE)
message(sprintf("Wrote %d classified rows to investor_risk_bands", nrow(out)))
dbDisconnect(con)

rationale: Approve tier (Step 1) is forced three times over: (a) it WRITES to a system of record — dbWriteTable(con, "investor_risk_bands", out, append=TRUE) appends to the production PortfolioWarehouse EDW, an authoritative store, which alone fails 5.3; and (c) it relies on probabilistic AI output (per-position risk bands) over fund data with NO human reviewing each output — the classify_band() result is written straight back to the warehouse unreviewed. Step 2 Lane-1 conditions fail decisively: 5.3 (writes to a source of truth via DBI::dbWriteTable), 5.4 (probabilistic logic without 100% human review — batch-scored and persisted), and 5.5 (a direct runtime AI call to the vendor host api.anthropic.com/v1/messages via httr::POST, not the approved same-origin /api/chat proxy). Section 6 also applies independently: it needs a persistent ODBC/DBI connection to a production SQL Server warehouse, which cannot live on the static shared host. The AI tool that may have BUILT the script is irrelevant — these are all runtime behaviors. Verdict: Lane 2, Approve-state.

title: Accrued-interest / day-count calculator (Go net/http server on :8080)
description: A small, idiomatic Go HTTP service exposing two JSON endpoints that compute day counts (ACT/360, ACT/365, 30/360) and accrued interest purely from numeric inputs the caller supplies. It does no AI, no database, no outbound network calls, and never touches client/fund data — the logic is fully deterministic arithmetic. The case probes whether the engine over-weights the clean, benign logic (which would read as Lane 1) and misses that the utility is a standalone server runtime (net/http + http.ListenAndServe on :8080) that must be run and hosted, not a static file — which Section 6 forces to Lane 2.
attack: Benign deterministic math tempts a Lane 1 verdict; the engine must catch that a net/http server runtime (Section 6) overrides clean logic and forces Lane 2.
rationale: Tier is Use: the utility is personal/general arithmetic on caller-supplied numbers, the builder reviews the output, and it does not write to any system of record, does not feed a relied-on deliverable, and uses no probabilistic logic — so none of the Approve-state triggers (a/b/c) fire. The logic itself satisfies the 5.x content gates: 5.2 (no client/fund data, only numbers), 5.3 (no writes), 5.4 (fully deterministic/Green), 5.5 (no runtime AI call), and 5.7 (no local persistence). The decisive condition is SECTION 6: the utility is a standalone server process — net/http with http.ListenAndServe on :8080 in cmd/daycount/main.go — that must be run and hosted as its own runtime, so it cannot live on the static shared host. Section 6 explicitly makes a Go web server Lane 2 even if its logic is deterministic and benign, which also breaks 5.6 (the static-host SSO model). Tier Use keeps it out of Approve, but the server runtime forces Lane 2, not Lane 1.

title: Vendor invoice intake API (ASP.NET minimal API + EF Core SaveChanges)
description: A C# ASP.NET Core minimal-API microservice that accepts vendor-invoice line items over HTTP and persists them to the AP SQL database through Entity Framework Core (_db.Invoices.Add(entity); await _db.SaveChangesAsync();). It is a long-running backend server (WebApplication.CreateBuilder / app.MapPost / app.Run) holding a persistent DbContext connection, and it commits an authoritative write to a system of record. No AI anywhere. Probes whether the engine recognizes an EF ORM persist as a §5.3 authoritative write even though there is no raw SQL INSERT keyword, and whether it flags the standing web-server runtime under §6.
attack: ORM write hidden behind EF .Add()/.SaveChangesAsync() with no raw SQL INSERT keyword, plus a backend server runtime, so a scanner keying on SQL/REST verbs could under-call both §5.3 and §6.
rationale: Two independent conditions force Lane 2, and the tier is Approve. (1) §5.3 / Step-1(a): the handler commits an authoritative write to a system of record — _db.Invoices.Add(entity) followed by await _db.SaveChangesAsync() is an EF Core ORM persist into the AP SQL ledger (ap_invoices). This is exactly an INSERT to an accounting source of truth, even though no raw SQL INSERT keyword appears; the ORM persist is the §5.3 write and makes the tier Approve. (2) §6: it is a standing backend web server — WebApplication.CreateBuilder / app.MapPost / app.Run with a persistent DbContext (UseSqlServer) connection — which cannot run on the shared static host regardless of logic. So §5.1 (tier is Approve, not Use) fails and §5.3 fails. No AI call exists, so §5.5 is not the deciding factor. Either the authoritative EF write or the backend-server runtime alone is sufficient for Lane 2; together with the system-of-record write the tier is Approve.

title: Notebook: GPT-4o per-holding commentary appended to reporting DB
description: A Jupyter notebook (.ipynb) used by fund ops to generate the quarterly position commentary pack. It imports openai and pandas, reads a fund positions CSV from a file share, calls openai.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', ...) once per holding to draft portfolio-manager commentary over real fund data, then appends every generated row straight into a production reporting database via df.to_sql('position_commentary', engine, if_exists='append'). No human reviews the model output before it is persisted. Probes whether the engine treats a notebook as harmless analysis or correctly sees the direct vendor AI call plus the authoritative DB write over fund data.

title: PostCapitalCall stored procedure — posts to capital account ledger and updates investor balances
description: A T-SQL stored procedure that posts a capital call: it INSERTs a row into dbo.CapitalAccountLedger and UPDATEs dbo.InvestorBalances for the given investor and amount, inside a transaction. This is a direct write to a fund system of record expressed purely in SQL. The case probes whether the classifier recognizes authoritative INSERT/UPDATE persistence (Approve-state, 5.3) even when there is no application server, no AI, no network call, and the logic is fully deterministic — i.e. that "deterministic + benign + no backend code" does not buy a light path when the operation mutates a ledger of record.
attack: Tests whether the engine flags a pure-SQL INSERT/UPDATE to a ledger/balance system of record as an Approve-state write, rather than excusing it because the logic is deterministic and there is no web server, AI, or outbound call.
rationale: STEP 1 risk tier = Approve-state under condition (a): the procedure performs authoritative writes to a fund system of record — INSERT INTO dbo.CapitalAccountLedger (a capital account ledger) and UPDATE dbo.InvestorBalances SET Balance = Balance + @Amount (investor capital records). These are exactly the ledger/positions/capital-record mutations the policy names. The tier always wins. STEP 2: it fails 5.1 (tier is Approve, not Use, which forces Lane 2) and fails 5.3 (it writes to a source of truth / production system). It also fails 5.2 because client/fund data is not merely read/formatted — it is persisted and the resulting balance is the authoritative figure relied on downstream. The absence of AI (5.4/5.5 are deterministic and clean) and the absence of outbound network calls do NOT rescue it: a single Approve-state authoritative write is dispositive. Independently, SECTION 6 also points to Lane 2 — a stored procedure that mutates ledger and balance tables needs a live, persistent connection to an authoritative database to run, which the static shared host cannot provide. Both the tier (5.1/5.3) and the runtime-dependency analysis (Section 6) land on Lane 2 / Approve.

title: AI Governance Committee landing page (brochure-ware, keyword-baited)
description: A purely static internal landing page for the firm's AI Governance Committee: index.html + styles.css + a tiny main.js whose only behavior is a deterministic FAQ accordion toggle (addEventListener click). The page text and CSS class names are deliberately saturated with high-signal governance vocabulary ('OpenAI', 'Anthropic', 'restricted access', 'portfolio reviews', 'NAV oversight', 'investor data policy', class names like restricted-notice and nav-oversight). There is no fetch, no AI model call, no write, no storage, no outbound network request, no backend. It probes whether the classifier scores on lexical bait (vendor names + finance/data nouns in copy and selectors) rather than on actual runtime behavior.
attack: Lexical false-positive: vendor/finance keywords ('OpenAI', 'Anthropic', 'NAV', 'portfolio', 'restricted', 'investor data') appear only in display text, comments, and CSS class names, with zero corresponding fetch/AI/write/storage behavior.

rationale: Tier (Step 1) is Use: the page performs no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/PUT/PATCH and no ORM persist to any system of record (1a does not fire); it produces no client deliverable, report, reconciliation, or filing (1b does not fire) -- it is informational brochure-ware; and it runs no probabilistic/AI/ML extraction over fund data (1c does not fire). It is general internal content reviewed by its builders. Lane 1 (Step 2) holds on every 5.x condition: 5.1 tier is Use; 5.2 general data only (no client/fund data is read or processed at all -- the 'portfolio', 'NAV', 'investor data', 'OpenAI', and 'Anthropic' tokens appear only in display copy, an HTML comment, and CSS class names like restricted-notice / nav-oversight, which the policy explicitly states are NOT real signals); 5.3 no writes to any source of truth; 5.4 logic is deterministic Green (a single addEventListener click handler that toggles the hidden attribute -- no probabilistic logic); 5.5 no runtime AI model call of any kind, not even the enterprise proxy; 5.6 no fetch/XHR, no outbound network calls, no third-party CDN scripts (main.js and styles.css are same-origin relative assets); 5.7 no localStorage/sessionStorage/cookies, so no local persistence of sensitive data. Section 6 does not apply: there is no backend, server runtime, integration, persistent data connection, or own authentication -- three static files that drop onto the shared static host unchanged. The case probes whether the classifier fires on lexical bait (vendor names plus finance/data nouns in text and selectors) instead of actual runtime behavior; honest application of the policy yields a clean Lane 1 / Use.

title: Python CLI batch-summarizes .txt files via string-assembled OpenAI host into summaries.csv
description: A Python CLI a developer wrote to summarize a folder of investor memo/.txt files. It never writes the literal vendor host: the URL is built by concatenation ("https://api." + "open" + "ai" + ".com/v1/chat/completions"), so a scanner grepping for the intact string api.openai.com finds nothing. But the OpenAI Chat Completions payload is fully intact in the request body ({"model": "gpt-4o", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]}) and it is POSTed with requests.post(base, json=body, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {key}"}). It globs *.txt, summarizes each, and writes summaries.csv with no human review. This probes whether the engine fingerprints a direct vendor AI call by the model+messages[role,content] payload shape (and a Bearer-key POST) rather than by the easily-obfuscated host string.
attack: Vendor AI host hidden via string concatenation so a literal-URL grep misses it; the AI call must be caught by the intact model+messages[role,content] request-body fingerprint instead.

files.snippet:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Batch-summarize a folder of investor memo .txt files into summaries.csv."""
import os
import csv
import glob
import requests

# host assembled at runtime so it doesn't show up in a naive scan
base = "https://api." + "open" + "ai" + ".com/v1/chat/completions"
key = os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"]


def summarize(text):
    prompt = "Summarize this investor memo in 2 sentences:\n\n" + text
    body = {"model": "gpt-4o", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]}
    resp = requests.post(base, json=body, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {key}"})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()


def main(folder="memos"):
    rows = []
    for path in glob.glob(os.path.join(folder, "*.txt")):
        with open(path, encoding="utf-8") as fh:
            rows.append((os.path.basename(path), summarize(fh.read())))
    with open("summaries.csv", "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as out:
        writer = csv.writer(out)
        writer.writerow(["file", "summary"])
        writer.writerows(rows)
    print(f"Wrote {len(rows)} summaries to summaries.csv")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

rationale: Lane 2, Approve-state. Tier is Approve via Step-1(c): the CLI relies on probabilistic AI output (gpt-4o) over fund/client memo text and writes every result straight to summaries.csv with NO human reviewing any output. Lane: fails 5.5 because it makes a direct runtime AI call to the OpenAI vendor host, NOT the approved relative /api/chat proxy — the host is obfuscated by concatenation ("https://api."+"open"+"ai"+".com/v1/chat/completions") so a literal api.openai.com grep misses it, but the call is unmistakable from the intact request-body fingerprint ("model": "gpt-4o" plus messages:[{role,content}]) and the Bearer-key requests.post. It also fails 5.4: probabilistic logic (Yellow) without 100% human review — batch-exported to CSV unreviewed. Step-1(c) and 5.4/5.5 each independently force Lane 2; the obfuscated host is the analysis weakness under test.

Strictly READ-ONLY (no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), no AI, no outbound network calls. The classification hinges entirely on the fact that it is a server-side PHP app holding a persistent, live connection to a production system of record over client/fund data.
attack: Probes whether the engine over-weights 'read-only + deterministic + no AI' and misses that a server-side PHP app with a persistent live production DB connection is a backend that cannot use the static shared host (Section 6).

This is a deployed server-side PHP app (a PHP web process) that opens a live, persistent PDO connection (PDO::ATTR_PERSISTENT => true) to the PRODUCTION fund-admin database (host=dbprod, dbname=fundadmin). A server runtime plus a persistent live production data connection cannot run on the static shared host, so Section 6 forces Lane 2 on its own -- even though the logic is deterministic (Green) and benign. On the Section 5 audit it also fails 5.6: that condition requires no backend/own server runtime and a static-hostable shape, which a PHP server with a live DB link does not meet. The tier is REGISTER, not Approve: it is shared/relied on by staff (more than the builder), but it is strictly READ-ONLY -- the SELECT-only queries mean there is NO write to a system of record (5.3 is satisfied for the write test), it produces no relied-on client deliverable, and there is no probabilistic/AI output, so none of the three Approve gates (authoritative write, deliverable, unreviewed probabilistic) fire. Key trap for the classifier: read-only + deterministic + no AI + no outbound is NOT enough for Lane 1 -- the persistent server-side connection to a live production DB is itself the Lane 2 signal under Section 6. Tier is Register (Lane 2 / Isolated Hosting), not Approve.

title: Approved /api/chat proxy co-located with a host-obfuscated direct vendor AI call (Lane 2)
description: A tool that legitimately uses the enterprise /api/chat proxy for one feature AND, in a separate module, makes a host-obfuscated direct OpenAI call (host assembled as 'op'+'enai', endpoint in a variable, full chat-completions payload). The direct call is detectable only by the AI payload fingerprint; the proxy must not cloak it.
rationale: Section 5.5 permits a runtime AI call only through the approved same-origin enterprise proxy. Here the /api/chat call qualifies, but embed.js makes a SECOND, genuinely direct vendor call whose host and SDK are obfuscated so the literal-host and SDK detectors miss it; only the AI payload fingerprint (model + chat messages) catches it. The proxy veto must be co-scoped: a proxy call in chat.js cannot license a direct call in embed.js. embed.js carries an AI payload AND a non-/api network call (fetch to a variable endpoint), so it is a direct AI call and fails 5.5 -> Lane 2. Regression guard for the proxy-cloak evasion.

title: Legit /api/chat proxy app that also reads a same-origin config via a variable URL (stays Lane 1)
description: The positive control for the co-scoped proxy fix: a tool that routes its AI through the approved /api/chat proxy on general data AND, in a separate module, does a benign config fetch to a variable URL. The benign non-proxy fetch must NOT be mistaken for a direct AI destination and must not over-escalate.
rationale: The only AI payload (messages) lives in app.js and goes to the approved /api/chat proxy, so 5.5 passes via the named exception. config.js makes a non-proxy fetch but carries no AI payload, so it is not a direct AI destination — the co-scoped proxy fix must not treat an unrelated config fetch as an AI call. General data, no writes, no restricted scope, output reviewed -> every 5.x condition holds -> Lane 1. This guards against the co-scoped fix over-escalating legitimate proxy apps.

title: Real ORM write hidden behind six benign builder/UI .create()/.save() calls (Lane 2)

rationale: db.js performs a real ORM write (prisma.records.create) — a write to a system of record (5.3) that auto-defaults the write target to authoritative, an Approve-state trigger. The six benign components only call generic builder methods (.create()/.save()/.update()) that are deliberately excluded by the tightened DB_ORM_WRITE regex. The bug class this guards: those benign matches fill the broad db-write signal's 6-slot evidence cap and break the per-file scan loop, so the genuine prisma write in the seventh file is never recorded; testing the precise regex over capped evidence then misses it (false Lane 1). Grepping the full comment-stripped corpus fixes it -> Lane 2.
